---
title: "Module 4: Subqueries and CTEs"
subtitle: "Organizing multi-step queries"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, default-fonts]
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
---

```{css, echo=FALSE}
.remark-code, .remark-inline-code { font-size: 80%; }
.remark-slide-content { padding: 1em 2em; }
.write-ref {
  position: absolute;
  bottom: 12px;
  right: 90px;
  background: #f0f8ff;
  border: 1px solid #aac;
  border-radius: 4px;
  padding: 1px 6px;
  font-size: 11px;
  text-decoration: none;
  color: #336;
  font-weight: bold;
  z-index: 100;
}
.write-ref:hover { background: #ddeeff; text-decoration: none; }
.small { font-size: 80%; }
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.align = "center", message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
library(DBI); library(RSQLite); library(tidyverse)
con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "../data/uber.sqlite")
knitr::opts_chunk$set(connection = "con")
```

# Course Map

<a href="../module-01/slides.html#schema" style="font-size:13px;background:#f0f8ff;border:1px solid #aac;border-radius:4px;padding:2px 8px;text-decoration:none;color:#336;">Schema ref</a>

<table>
<tr><th>#</th><th>Module</th><th>Status</th></tr>
<tr><td>1</td><td><a href="../module-01/slides.html">SELECT, WHERE, Aggregates</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td>2</td><td><a href="../module-02/slides.html">JOINs</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td>3</td><td><a href="../module-03/slides.html">GROUP BY, HAVING, Conversion Funnels</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td><b>4</b></td><td><b>Subqueries and CTEs</b> <i>(you are here)</i></td><td>← current</td></tr>
<tr><td>5</td><td>Window Functions</td><td>upcoming</td></tr>
</table>

---

# Why You Need Them

Once a query gets past ~5 lines, you need a way to organize intermediate results. SQL gives you two:

--

- **Subqueries** — a query inside another query
- **Common Table Expressions (CTEs)** — named subqueries, top-to-bottom

--

Both compile to similar plans. **Default to CTEs** — they're more readable, easier to debug, and let you build complex queries in clean steps.

---

# Three Subquery Flavors

**Scalar:** returns one value, used like a constant.

```sql
SELECT * FROM rides WHERE fare_usd > (SELECT AVG(fare_usd) FROM rides);
```

--

**Table:** returns a table, used in `FROM`.

```sql
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT driver_id, COUNT(*) AS n FROM rides GROUP BY driver_id
) t WHERE t.n > 100;
```

--

**Correlated:** references the outer query, runs once per outer row. Convenient but slow.

```sql
SELECT d.*, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM rides r WHERE r.driver_id = d.driver_id) AS n
FROM   drivers d;
```

---

# CTEs

```sql
WITH driver_rides AS (
  SELECT driver_id, COUNT(*) AS n_rides
  FROM   rides
  GROUP BY driver_id
)
SELECT * FROM driver_rides WHERE n_rides > 100;
```

--

Multiple CTEs:

```sql
WITH
  cte1 AS (...),
  cte2 AS (...),
  cte3 AS (...)
SELECT * FROM cte3;
```

--

Each CTE = one logical step. Read top-to-bottom like a script.

---

# Q: For each rider, find the date of their first ride and the city it was in.

<a class="write-ref" href="../module-03/slides.html#write-order">WO</a>
*Hint: CTE to find `MIN(started_at)` per rider, then JOIN back to get the city.*

--

```{sql first, eval=TRUE}
WITH first_ride AS (
  SELECT req.rider_id, MIN(r.started_at) AS first_started_at
  FROM   rides r
  JOIN   requests req ON r.request_id = req.request_id
  GROUP BY req.rider_id
)
SELECT fr.rider_id, fr.first_started_at, c.name AS first_city
FROM   first_ride fr
JOIN   rides r ON r.started_at = fr.first_started_at
JOIN   requests req ON r.request_id = req.request_id
JOIN   neighborhoods n ON req.pickup_nbhd_id = n.nbhd_id
JOIN   cities c ON n.city_id = c.city_id
LIMIT 5;
```

---

# Q: Build a December funnel (request → ride → 5-star) using a chain of CTEs.

<a class="write-ref" href="../module-03/slides.html#write-order">WO</a>
--

```sql
WITH dec_requests AS (
  SELECT request_id, rider_id FROM requests
  WHERE date(requested_at) BETWEEN '2025-12-01' AND '2025-12-31'
),
dec_rides AS (
  SELECT dr.*, r.ride_id, r.rider_rating
  FROM dec_requests dr LEFT JOIN rides r ON dr.request_id = r.request_id
),
funnel AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS n_requests,
    SUM(CASE WHEN ride_id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_completed,
    SUM(CASE WHEN rider_rating = 5 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_5star
  FROM dec_rides
)
SELECT * FROM funnel;
```

--

The pattern: each CTE is a step. Easy to test in isolation.

---

# Q: Find riders who were active in each of the last 4 weeks of December.

<a class="write-ref" href="../module-03/slides.html#write-order">WO</a>
*Hint: collapse requests to rider × week, then `HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT week) >= 4`.*

--

```{sql active-n, eval=TRUE}
WITH active AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    rider_id,
    strftime('%Y-%W', requested_at) AS week
  FROM   requests
  WHERE  date(requested_at) BETWEEN '2025-12-01' AND '2025-12-31'
)
SELECT rider_id, COUNT(DISTINCT week) AS weeks_active
FROM   active
GROUP BY rider_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT week) >= 4
LIMIT 5;
```

--

Trick: collapse each request to its week, then `HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT week) = N`.

---

# CTE vs Subquery

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Reference the same intermediate twice | **CTE** |
| ≥ 3 logical steps | **CTE** |
| Need a single value in WHERE | **Scalar subquery** |
| One-line intermediate | **Subquery** |
| Need recursion | **Recursive CTE** |

--

In modern SQL, **default to CTEs**.

---

# Common Traps

**1.** Forgetting to JOIN back to the original after a CTE. The CTE has the rider_id and date, but you also wanted the city — you need a second join.

--

**2.** CTEs that aren't materialized. In Postgres < v12 they were materialized; after, they're inlined. SQLite inlines them. Don't expect a CTE to act as a memoization cache.

--

**3.** Naming collisions. `WITH rides AS (...)` shadows the real `rides` table. Be careful.

---
class: inverse

# The Key Takeaways

<br>

### 1. CTEs let you write 30-line queries as a chain of named steps. Default to them.

--

<br>

### 2. The first/last pattern: a CTE finds the extremum, then a join attaches the rest of the row.

--

<br>

### 3. Module 5 (window functions) replaces many of these patterns with one-liners — but the CTE approach works in any dialect.

---

# Course Map

<a href="../module-01/slides.html#schema" style="font-size:13px;background:#f0f8ff;border:1px solid #aac;border-radius:4px;padding:2px 8px;text-decoration:none;color:#336;">Schema ref</a>

<table>
<tr><th>#</th><th>Module</th><th>Status</th></tr>
<tr><td>1</td><td><a href="../module-01/slides.html">SELECT, WHERE, Aggregates</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td>2</td><td><a href="../module-02/slides.html">JOINs</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td>3</td><td><a href="../module-03/slides.html">GROUP BY, HAVING, Conversion Funnels</a></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td>4</td><td>Subqueries and CTEs <i>(just finished)</i></td><td>✓ done</td></tr>
<tr><td><b>5</b></td><td><b>Window Functions</b></td><td>next</td></tr>
</table>
